cacheKey.userContent:
[Source Text]
--- behavioral_review_critiques.md ---
\# Decision: Behavioral Review System

\*\*Status\*\*: Proposed — awaiting discussion with collaborators    
\*\*Date\*\*: 2026-03-11    
\*\*Context\*\*: AI-assisted development has increased author commit velocity to 10-100+ commits/day. The traditional code review process doesn't scale — reviewer effort grows linearly with commit volume. We need a review model where reviewer effort grows sublinearly.

\#\# The core question

What does "code review" mean when most code is AI-generated and the author's primary contribution is direction, verification, and quality control?

\#\# What review is for (in this context)

We identified three purposes of review, in priority order:

1\. \*\*Keep co-developers up to date\*\* on what the product does and how it works. Review is a communication mechanism — the reviewer should come away understanding the current state of the product.

2\. \*\*Ensure new users can onboard efficiently.\*\* The artifacts produced by review should help someone unfamiliar with the tool understand what it does and how to use it.

3\. \*\*Catch problems the author missed.\*\* This is the traditional purpose of review, but in an AI-assisted workflow, it should be the \*last\* line of defense, not the first. Correctness and code quality should be verified by the author (via testing, linting, automated review tools, multi-model review) before the PR is opened.

Notably absent: reviewing code for style, elegance, or implementation approach. These are author-side concerns, enforced by automated tooling and the author's own judgment.

\#\# What this changes about the reviewer's role

The reviewer's job shifts from "verify this code is correct" to "experience the product as a user and confirm the behavior matches expectations." This is closer to QA or user acceptance testing than traditional code review.

The reviewer should be able to:  
\- Work through a structured checklist of behaviors to verify  
\- Provide feedback on UX, workflow, and feature design (things automated tools can't catch)  
\- Approve or flag items at the granularity of individual features/commits  
\- Come away from the review session understanding what changed and why

The reviewer should NOT need to:  
\- Read implementation code line-by-line  
\- Verify correctness of logic (that's what tests are for)  
\- Understand the libraries or language features used  
\- Review every commit in a large batch to approve the ones they've verified

\#\# Proposed approach

A \*\*behavioral review system\*\* combining:

\#\#\# 1\. A guided walkthrough (serves both review and onboarding)

A living document (or lightweight app) that presents a structured list of product behaviors organized by feature area. Each item describes something the user can do and what they should observe:

\> \*\*Source Input → File Upload\*\*    
\> Upload a .pdf file using the paperclip icon. The filename should appear below the input area and the text content should populate the left panel within a few seconds.

For review: the reviewer works through recent additions to this list, verifying each behavior in the running app. Checking a box means "I verified this works and the experience is acceptable."

For onboarding: a new user works through the full list (or a relevant subset) to learn the product's capabilities. The same descriptions that tell a reviewer what to check tell a new user what they can do.

The walkthrough is curated by the author — not every commit produces a user-facing walkthrough item. Developer-facing checks (component renders, API returns 200, test passes) are handled separately by CI.

\#\#\# 2\. Automated verification as the first pass

Before the reviewer sees anything:  
\- CI runs the full test suite (unit, integration, behavioral tests where they exist)  
\- Linting and type checking pass  
\- Optionally: an automated code review tool (CodeRabbit, multi-model review, or similar) runs and its findings are resolved or acknowledged

This means the reviewer can trust that basic correctness has been verified. Their job is to catch things automation can't: confusing UX, features that technically work but feel wrong, missing functionality that wasn't specified.

\#\#\# 3\. Commit-level mapping for partial merge

Each walkthrough item maps to one or more commits (or a commit range). When the reviewer verifies a batch of items, the corresponding commits can be merged to main. Items not yet reviewed remain on the queue for the next review session.

This allows the reviewer to do as much or as little as they have time for in a session, and progress is never blocked on reviewing the entire batch.

\#\# Open questions for discussion

These need input from all collaborators before implementation:

\#\#\# What counts as "reviewed"?

\- Is checking the behavioral box sufficient, or does the reviewer also need to look at the PR diff?  
\- Can a reviewer approve items they haven't personally verified if CI passes all tests? (i.e., is automated testing sufficient for some categories of changes?)  
\- What happens when a behavioral check fails — does it block only that commit, or everything after it?

\#\#\# How much author effort is appropriate?

\- Writing behavioral checklist items per commit is additional author work. Is this acceptable? (Current position: yes, because it shifts burden from reviewer to author and produces onboarding documentation as a byproduct.)  
\- Should the AI agent generate draft checklist items that the author curates, or should the author write them manually?

\#\#\# Walkthrough format and hosting

\- Is a markdown file in the repo sufficient, or does this need to be an interactive app?  
\- If an app: should it be a route in the product itself, a separate tool, or a GitHub Pages site?  
\- How should it handle branching for different user roles or experience levels?

\#\#\# Social contract

\- Does every collaborator agree that "review \= verify behavior" rather than "review \= read code"?  
\- Is there a category of change that still requires traditional code review? (Security-sensitive changes? Architectural decisions? Infrastructure?)  
\- How do we handle disagreements about product direction surfaced during behavioral review?

\#\#\# How is Trust Maintained

\- In order to support the social contract, reviewers must trust the completeness and correctness of automated verification.  
\- How is test coverage calculated?  
\- How does the reviewer know that all code changes are relevant to the behavior being verified?

\#\#\# Relationship to existing workflows

\- How does this interact with the PR preparation workflow? (PR descriptions may become less important if the walkthrough is the primary review artifact.)  
\- Does the branch strategy change? (Partial merge by commit may work better with non-squash merge to main.)  
\- How does the review queue interact with the timezone gap? (Reviewer works through the queue during their day, author sees results and feedback the next morning.)

\#\# Alternatives considered

\- \*\*Traditional PR review at scale\*\*: Doesn't work. Reviewer effort is linear with commit count and the reviewer can't meaningfully assess AI-generated code in unfamiliar libraries.  
\- \*\*Sidecar manifest (YAML/JSON review queue)\*\*: Simpler but doesn't produce the onboarding byproduct. Good fallback if the walkthrough approach is too heavy.  
\- \*\*Living markdown checklist (single doc for review \+ onboarding)\*\*: Conflates two audiences with different needs. Review items are ephemeral; onboarding items are permanent. Forcing them into one document means compromising on both.  
\- \*\*Fully automated (tests only, no human review)\*\*: Misses the communication purpose of review. The reviewer needs to \*experience\* the product to stay current on what it does.

\#\# Next steps

1\. Circulate this document to all collaborators for feedback  
2\. Discuss open questions, especially the social contract items  
3\. Once there's agreement on what "review" means, design the specific tooling (walkthrough format, CI integration, commit mapping)

—------

Gemini critique+repair:

Here are proposed mitigations and solutions for the critiques, structured so you can easily adapt them into the "Open questions for discussion" or "Proposed approach" sections of your document.

### **1\. Mitigating the Git Workflow & Dependency Reality**

**The Problem:** Partial merging by commit risks breaking `main` due to interdependent code changes. **The Solution: Shift to Feature-Flag or PR-Level Mapping** Instead of mapping behavioral checks to individual commits, map them to feature branches or explicitly use feature flags.

* **Feature Flags:** The author merges the entire batch of commits to `main` hidden behind a feature flag. The reviewer toggles the flag in a staging environment, verifies the behavior, and then approves the flag's release.  
* **Squash/Batch PRs:** Require the author to group their 10-100+ daily commits into cohesive, independently verifiable Pull Requests. The reviewer approves the *feature*, not the commit sequence.

### **2\. Mitigating Architecture and Code Rot**

**The Problem:** Entirely removing human review of "style, elegance, or implementation approach" guarantees the rapid accumulation of unmaintainable AI slop. **The Solution: A Two-Tiered Review Strategy (Behavioral \+ Architectural)** Establish clear boundaries for what qualifies for pure Behavioral Review versus what requires Architectural Review.

* **Behavioral Only:** UI tweaks, localized component logic, copy changes, and standard CRUD operations.  
* **Architectural Review Required:** Changes to database schemas, API boundaries, new system dependencies, or core data models. This ensures human engineering oversight remains where the cost of AI hallucination or poor optimization is highest.

### **3\. Mitigating the Circular Validation Trap**

**The Problem:** Trusting automated tests when the same AI wrote both the feature and the tests creates blind spots for logic and security flaws. **The Solution: Multi-Model Auditing & Intent-Based Testing**

* **Adversarial AI Review:** Explicitly implement the "multi-model review" you mentioned by using a different, specialized LLM strictly configured to act as an adversarial security and logic auditor.  
* **Human-Authored Test Scenarios:** The AI can write the test code, but the author must explicitly define the test *cases* (especially edge cases and failure modes) to break the circular logic loop.

### **4\. Mitigating the Velocity Paradox**

**The Problem:** Forcing the author to manually write checklist items for a high volume of commits destroys the speed gained by using AI in the first place. **The Solution: AI-Drafted, Human-Curated Checklists** Directly answer your open question about author effort by automating the creation of the review artifact.

* Have an AI agent analyze the diffs and automatically generate the draft behavioral checklist.  
* The author's job shifts to simply reviewing, pruning, and approving the generated checklist before passing it to the reviewer. This keeps author effort sublinear.

### **5\. Mitigating the Documentation Contradiction**

**The Problem:** Trying to make one document serve as both an ephemeral review checklist and a permanent onboarding guide will result in an artifact that fails at both. **The Solution: Fork the Artifacts**

* **The Review Queue (Ephemeral):** A temporary checklist generated per PR that includes happy paths, edge cases, and error states specifically for the reviewer. Once verified, it is archived.  
* **The Onboarding Walkthrough (Permanent):** A high-level, curated guide. When a PR is merged, the author decides if the new feature warrants adding a line to the permanent Onboarding Walkthrough. This keeps the onboarding document clean and focused only on core user flows.

—-----------

Claude critique+repair:

## **Divergent Design: Critiques of the Behavioral Review System**

### **1\. Diverge — generate many critical perspectives**

1. **The "nobody reads the walkthrough" critique.** Behavioral checklists become rote checkbox-ticking within weeks. Reviewers check boxes without actually testing, the same way people click "I agree" on terms of service. The system degrades into rubber-stamping with extra steps.  
2. **The "hidden rot" critique.** By explicitly deprioritizing code review, you lose the only mechanism for catching architectural decay. AI-generated code accumulates technical debt that no human ever reads. Six months later, the codebase is unmaintainable — not because any single behavior is wrong, but because the internal structure is a mess.  
3. **The "tests can't catch what you don't specify" critique.** Behavioral tests verify what you thought to test. The most dangerous bugs are in behaviors you didn't think to specify. Traditional code review catches these by accident — a reviewer says "wait, what happens if X?" while reading the implementation. Behavioral review systematically eliminates this serendipitous bug-finding.  
4. **The "author becomes a single point of failure" critique.** The author is now responsible for correctness (tests), quality (automated tools), review artifact creation (walkthrough items), AND product direction. If the author makes a systematic error — misunderstands a requirement, uses a library wrong, introduces a subtle security flaw — there's no checkpoint that catches it. The reviewer is only checking behaviors the author defined.  
5. **The "onboarding doc is a lie" critique.** The walkthrough describes what the product *should* do. As the product evolves, walkthrough items go stale. Nobody updates "upload a PDF and see text appear" when the PDF parsing changes or breaks. The onboarding doc becomes a source of confusion rather than help, and maintaining it is a second job nobody signed up for.  
6. **The "this doesn't generalize" critique.** This system works when you have one fast author and one async reviewer. Add a third developer and the model breaks — who writes the walkthrough items? Who curates the onboarding guide? The process is optimized for a team of two and will need to be redesigned at three.  
7. **The "velocity is the problem, not the solution" critique.** 100 commits/day sounds productive but may indicate insufficient thinking before coding. The review bottleneck is a natural backpressure signal that forces prioritization. Removing it might mean you ship 100 features nobody needed instead of 10 features that matter. The system optimizes for throughput when it should optimize for impact.  
8. **The "false confidence from CI" critique.** "CI passes, so correctness is verified" only works if test coverage is comprehensive and tests are correct. AI-generated tests often test the happy path, miss edge cases, and can be subtly tautological (testing that the code does what it does, not that it does what it should). Trusting CI as the correctness layer requires test quality that's hard to achieve and harder to maintain.  
9. **The "reviewer loses context" critique.** In traditional review, the reviewer builds a mental model of the code by reading it. In behavioral review, the reviewer only knows what the product does, not how. When they need to contribute code later, they're starting from scratch. The communication purpose of review (keeping co-developers up to date) is only half-served — they know the *what* but not the *how*.  
10. **The "partial merge creates integration nightmares" critique.** If the reviewer approves commits 1-7 of 20 and those get merged, commits 8-20 are now based on a partially-merged state. If commit 8 depends on something in commit 5 that the reviewer flagged and you changed, your remaining branch is in a weird state. The bookkeeping to track what's merged, what's pending, and what's invalidated by feedback gets complex fast.  
11. **The "this is just QA with extra steps" critique.** You've described a QA process and called it code review. That's fine — but QA is a different skill than code review, and the people you're asking to do it may not be good at it or interested in it. If your coworker signed up to be a co-developer and you're asking them to be a QA tester, there's a mismatch.  
12. **The "walkthrough item quality" problem.** Writing good behavioral verification steps is a skill. Bad ones are either too vague ("verify the upload works") or too specific ("click the 3rd button from the left, observe the hex color is `#f5f5dc`"). The AI agent will generate mediocre ones. The author has to curate them. This curation is a non-trivial ongoing cost that's easy to underestimate.

### **2\. Diagnose — which critiques are real threats vs. manageable risks**

**Hard threats** (could cause the system to fail):

* **\#1 (rubber-stamping)**: This is the biggest social risk. If the reviewer doesn't actually test behaviors, the system provides no value. Mitigation depends on the reviewer being genuinely engaged, which is a people problem, not a tooling problem.  
* **\#4 (single point of failure)**: Real and structural. The author controls what gets tested and what gets reviewed. There's no independent check on "are we testing the right things?" This is partially mitigated by the reviewer's ability to flag missing behaviors, but only if they have enough product context to know what's missing.  
* **\#3 (can't catch unspecified bugs)**: Real, but the alternative (code review that the reviewer can't meaningfully do because the code is AI-generated in unfamiliar libraries) doesn't catch them either. This is an honest tradeoff, not a flaw — but it should be stated explicitly.

**Serious but mitigable:**

* **\#2 (hidden rot)**: Real long-term risk. Mitigated by periodic architectural review sessions (maybe monthly?) where someone does actually read the code structure. Doesn't need to happen per-commit, but needs to happen.  
* **\#8 (false CI confidence)**: Real. Mitigated by test coverage metrics, mutation testing, and the author treating test quality as a first-class responsibility. Should be called out in the decision doc as an ongoing obligation.  
* **\#9 (reviewer loses how-context)**: Partially real. The `docs/working/` research docs help here — they document the *how* even if the reviewer isn't reading code. Also mitigated by the decision records and thoughts directories.  
* **\#10 (partial merge complexity)**: Real operational complexity. Mitigated by clean commit ordering (independent commits first, dependent ones later) and accepting that sometimes the reviewer approves the whole batch or nothing.

**Valid but lower priority:**

* **\#5 (stale onboarding docs)**: Real but solvable with maintenance discipline. Same problem as any documentation — it rots if you don't tend it.  
* **\#6 (doesn't generalize to 3+ devs)**: True, but premature to worry about. Solve the two-person problem now.  
* **\#7 (velocity ≠ value)**: Philosophically valid, but the decision doc already frames this as a process problem, not a "ship everything" mandate. The reviewer's behavioral feedback serves as the prioritization signal — if they flag features as unnecessary, that's useful.  
* **\#11 (QA vs. review mismatch)**: Worth discussing with the coworker directly. If they want to do code review and not QA, the system needs adjustment.  
* **\#12 (walkthrough item quality)**: Real ongoing cost, but the author is already writing PR descriptions. This redirects that effort, not adds to it.

### **3\. Recommendations for the decision doc**

Based on these critiques, I'd suggest adding the following to the decision doc before circulating:

**Explicitly acknowledge the tradeoffs:**

* This system trades serendipitous bug-finding (from code review) for scalable behavioral verification. That's a real loss. It's accepted because the alternative (code review of AI-generated code in unfamiliar libraries) provides the *appearance* of that benefit without the reality.  
* The author becomes the single point of failure for correctness. This is mitigated by automated tooling but not eliminated. The team should be honest about this risk.

**Add to open questions:**

* Does the reviewer want to do behavioral verification, or do they want to read code? (The \#11 critique.) This needs to be asked directly, not assumed.  
* Should there be periodic (monthly?) architectural review sessions where someone actually reads the code structure? (The \#2 critique.)  
* What's the plan for test quality assurance? Coverage metrics? Mutation testing? Who watches the watchmen? (The \#8 critique.)

**Add a "risks and limitations" section** covering \#1 (rubber-stamping), \#3 (unspecified bugs), \#4 (single point of failure), and \#10 (partial merge complexity) so that everyone going in understands what this system does *not* catch.

—-----------

ChatGPT critique+repair

This is a thoughtful document with a clear thesis and a real problem statement. I’ll critique it from multiple angles: \*\*conceptual clarity, incentives, scalability, failure modes, developer psychology, security, governance, workflow mechanics, documentation, and long-term evolution.\*\* I’ll also point out \*\*implicit assumptions\*\* and \*\*places where collaborators might push back\*\*.

\---

\# 1\. Core Problem Framing

\#\#\# Strengths

\* The document identifies a \*\*real structural shift\*\*: AI-assisted development breaks the traditional linear relationship between commits and reviewer capacity.  
\* The key question is well framed:

  \> What does code review mean when most code is AI-generated?

This is a legitimate paradigm change.

\#\#\# Potential Issues

\#\#\#\# 1\. Commit velocity ≠ review workload

The argument assumes that \*\*commit count drives review cost\*\*, but in practice reviewers evaluate \*\*semantic change\*\*, not commits.

Example:

\* 100 commits could be one logical feature.  
\* 1 commit could introduce a massive subsystem.

This weakens the framing slightly. Review cost scales with:

\* \*\*surface area of change\*\*  
\* \*\*risk\*\*  
\* \*\*complexity\*\*  
\* \*\*novelty\*\*

not commits.

\*\*Possible reframing\*\*

Instead of:

\> AI increases commit velocity

The stronger claim is:

\> AI increases \*\*change surface area per unit time\*\*, which breaks reviewer bandwidth.

\---

\# 2\. Redefinition of Code Review

Your three purposes:

1\. Keep co-developers up to date  
2\. Help onboarding  
3\. Catch problems

This is interesting but \*\*controversial\*\*.

\#\#\# Traditional industry framing

In many engineering orgs review primarily serves:

1\. \*\*Correctness\*\*  
2\. \*\*Safety\*\*  
3\. \*\*Maintainability\*\*  
4\. \*\*Knowledge sharing\*\*

Your ordering flips this dramatically.

\#\#\# Risk

Collaborators may object because:

\* correctness is treated as \*\*secondary\*\*  
\* review becomes \*\*experience validation\*\*

This implicitly assumes \*\*tests \+ automation are extremely reliable\*\*.

That assumption may not hold.

\---

\# 3\. Hidden Assumptions

Several strong assumptions underpin the system.

\#\#\# Assumption 1 — Test coverage is extremely high

Your system works only if:

\* tests capture most logic errors  
\* behavioral tests exist for key flows  
\* CI is trustworthy

Otherwise reviewers \*\*lose their main defense\*\*.

\#\#\# Assumption 2 — AI-generated code is correct enough

If AI produces subtle bugs:

\* logic bugs  
\* race conditions  
\* performance regressions

behavioral testing may not expose them.

\#\#\# Assumption 3 — Authors are trustworthy curators

The author chooses:

\* which behaviors appear in the walkthrough  
\* which commits map to which behaviors

This creates \*\*review surface manipulation risk\*\*.

Example failure mode:

\`\`\`  
behavior: "upload file works"  
commit bundle includes:  
\- upload feature  
\- analytics pipeline  
\- telemetry system  
\- experimental caching  
\`\`\`

Reviewer only verifies upload.

\---

\# 4\. Incentive Design

This is one of the most important critiques.

\#\#\# You shift effort from reviewer → author

Which is good in theory.

But you also give the \*\*author control over review scope\*\*.

This creates incentives to:

\* minimize walkthrough items  
\* bundle unrelated changes  
\* under-specify behaviors

Example:

Bad walkthrough item:

\> "Settings page works"

This hides huge implementation complexity.

\#\#\# Possible mitigation

Require:

\* \*\*minimum granularity\*\*  
\* \*\*automated diff coverage reporting\*\*

Example:

\`\`\`  
90% of changed files linked to walkthrough items  
\`\`\`

\---

\# 5\. Behavioral Review Model

The core concept:

\> Review behavior, not code.

This is powerful but incomplete.

\#\#\# Good for:

\* UX  
\* product features  
\* workflows  
\* regressions

\#\#\# Weak for:

\* performance  
\* security  
\* architecture  
\* data integrity  
\* concurrency  
\* scalability  
\* maintainability

Example bug types your system misses:

\* N+1 queries  
\* O(n2) algorithm  
\* memory leak  
\* incorrect caching  
\* race conditions  
\* security vulnerabilities

These rarely show up in walkthrough verification.

\---

\# 6\. Security Risks

Security review is largely eliminated.

Example issues reviewers normally catch:

\* unsafe deserialization  
\* SQL injection risk  
\* privilege escalation  
\* auth bypass  
\* secrets leakage  
\* SSRF vulnerabilities

These \*\*do not appear in behavior testing\*\*.

Your doc briefly mentions:

\> Security-sensitive changes?

But this is actually \*\*a major missing pillar\*\*.

Possible solution:

Introduce \*\*review classes\*\*:

| Change Type         | Review Mode            |  
| \------------------- | \---------------------- |  
| UI/UX features      | Behavioral             |  
| business logic      | Behavioral \+ diff scan |  
| security            | Mandatory code review  |  
| infrastructure      | Code review            |  
| database migrations | Code review            |

\---

\# 7\. Architecture Blindness

Your system encourages reviewers to \*\*ignore implementation\*\*.

Long-term risk:

\* architecture drift  
\* technical debt accumulation  
\* inconsistent patterns

Review normally acts as \*\*architecture governance\*\*.

Without that, teams often accumulate:

\* duplicated systems  
\* divergent abstractions  
\* framework misuse

AI may exacerbate this.

\---

\# 8\. Commit-Level Mapping

This is one of the most fragile parts.

You propose:

\> Each walkthrough item maps to commits.

Problems:

\#\#\# 1\. Commits rarely map cleanly to behavior

Typical feature touches:

\* UI  
\* backend  
\* schema  
\* tests  
\* configs

These are interdependent.

\#\#\# 2\. Partial merges can break builds

Example:

\`\`\`  
commit A: UI  
commit B: API  
commit C: schema  
\`\`\`

Reviewer approves UI item.

Merge A → build broken.

\#\#\# 3\. Git workflows aren't designed for behavioral slicing

You would need:

\* dependency tracking  
\* commit graph validation

This becomes complex tooling.

\---

\# 9\. Behavioral Checklist Maintenance

This becomes a \*\*second product spec\*\*.

Risks:

\#\#\# Drift

Behavior descriptions become outdated.

\#\#\# Duplication

Now you have:

\* product spec  
\* tests  
\* walkthrough doc

Three sources of truth.

\#\#\# Maintenance burden

Over time:

\* checklist grows huge  
\* items become stale  
\* onboarding gets overwhelming

Many projects suffer this with:

\* cucumber specs  
\* gherkin scenarios  
\* QA scripts

\---

\# 10\. Reviewer Cognitive Load

You claim reviewer effort becomes sublinear.

But it may instead become \*\*shifted cognitive load\*\*.

Traditional review:

\`\`\`  
skim diff  
focus on key logic  
\`\`\`

Behavioral review:

\`\`\`  
launch app  
perform flows  
observe UI  
compare expectations  
\`\`\`

This can actually be \*\*slower per feature\*\*.

Especially if:

\* environment setup required  
\* manual testing needed  
\* multiple states required

\---

\# 11\. Knowledge Transfer

You emphasize review as communication.

But behavioral verification provides \*\*very shallow understanding\*\*.

Example:

Reviewer knows:

\`\`\`  
"Upload PDF works"  
\`\`\`

But not:

\`\`\`  
\- where parsing happens  
\- what library is used  
\- where failures propagate  
\- how scaling works  
\`\`\`

This weakens \*\*bus factor resilience\*\*.

\---

\# 12\. Automation Trust Problem

Your document identifies this correctly:

\> reviewers must trust automated verification

But doesn't define \*\*how that trust is established\*\*.

Questions collaborators will ask:

\* minimum coverage thresholds?  
\* mutation testing?  
\* flaky test detection?  
\* runtime observability?

Without these, behavioral review becomes \*\*faith-based\*\*.

\---

\# 13\. Social Contract

This is arguably the most important section.

Your system only works if the team agrees:

\> "review ≠ reading code"

This is a \*\*huge cultural shift\*\*.

Many engineers believe code review is essential for:

\* craftsmanship  
\* mentorship  
\* shared standards

Removing it may trigger resistance.

\---

\# 14\. AI-Specific Considerations Missing

Since this system is motivated by AI coding, it should address:

\#\#\# AI failure patterns

AI often introduces:

\* hallucinated APIs  
\* incorrect edge cases  
\* inefficient implementations  
\* subtle logic errors

Behavior testing might not expose these.

\#\#\# AI architectural drift

AI tends to:

\* duplicate patterns  
\* introduce unnecessary abstractions  
\* misuse frameworks

Human review usually corrects this.

\---

\# 15\. Metrics Missing

The proposal would be stronger with measurable goals.

Example metrics:

\* reviewer minutes per change  
\* defects found post-merge  
\* onboarding time  
\* feature throughput

Without metrics, success will be hard to evaluate.

\---

\# 16\. Missing Middle Ground

The proposal frames the options as:

\* traditional review  
\* behavioral review

But there are hybrid approaches:

\#\#\# Risk-based review

\`\`\`  
low risk → behavioral  
medium → skim diff  
high → full review  
\`\`\`

\#\#\# Diff summarization

AI summarizes changes.

Reviewer reads summary \+ key diffs.

\#\#\# Architecture gates

Only structural changes require review.

\---

\# 17\. Scalability Concerns

The walkthrough could become enormous.

Example mature product:

\`\`\`  
1000+ behaviors  
\`\`\`

Even filtering by "recent changes" requires curation.

Over time:

\* list grows  
\* taxonomy needed  
\* navigation becomes hard

This becomes a \*\*product documentation system\*\*.

\---

\# 18\. Governance and Ownership

Who owns:

\* walkthrough quality?  
\* test completeness?  
\* automation reliability?

If the author controls everything, review loses independence.

Large teams often enforce:

\* separate QA  
\* independent reviewers  
\* security reviewers

\---

\# 19\. Tooling Complexity

Your approach requires significant tooling:

\* behavior registry  
\* commit mapping  
\* merge gating  
\* CI integration  
\* reviewer interface  
\* branching awareness

This is \*\*non-trivial infrastructure\*\*.

You may be underestimating implementation cost.

\---

\# 20\. Strongest Parts of the Proposal

To balance the critique, several ideas are genuinely strong.

\#\#\# 1\. Behavior-first review

Extremely valuable for:

\* product teams  
\* UX-heavy apps  
\* fast iteration environments

\#\#\# 2\. Author responsibility shift

Good principle:

\> the author proves correctness before review

Many teams need this.

\#\#\# 3\. Review as product exposure

For small teams this is excellent.

\#\#\# 4\. Onboarding artifact reuse

Dual-purpose documentation is clever.

\---

\# 21\. Most Likely Failure Modes

If implemented naïvely:

1\. \*\*Checklist gaming\*\*  
2\. \*\*Hidden complexity slipping through\*\*  
3\. \*\*architecture drift\*\*  
4\. \*\*security regressions\*\*  
5\. \*\*tooling maintenance burden\*\*  
6\. \*\*reviewer disengagement\*\*

\---

\# 22\. Key Missing Safeguards

The system likely needs:

\#\#\# Structural review triggers

Mandatory diff review for:

\* auth  
\* payments  
\* security  
\* infra  
\* migrations

\#\#\# Coverage validation

CI should verify:

\`\`\`  
changed code ↔ behaviors  
\`\`\`

\#\#\# Architecture oversight

Periodic design reviews.

\#\#\# Performance checks

Benchmarks in CI.

\---

\# 23\. Suggested Structural Improvement

Instead of replacing review entirely:

Create \*\*three layers\*\*.

\#\#\# Layer 1 — Automation

\* tests  
\* lint  
\* AI review

\#\#\# Layer 2 — Behavioral review

UX \+ functionality.

\#\#\# Layer 3 — Structural review

Only when triggered by:

\* architecture changes  
\* security  
\* core systems

This preserves scale while maintaining safety.

\---

\# 24\. Biggest Conceptual Risk

The document implicitly assumes:

\> Implementation quality no longer matters as much as behavior.

But long-term system health depends heavily on \*\*implementation quality\*\*.

Behavior alone doesn't capture:

\* maintainability  
\* extensibility  
\* performance

\---

\# 25\. Final Meta Critique

This document is strongest as:

\*\*a proposal for a new \*layer\* of review\*\*

not a \*\*replacement for code review\*\*.

Framing it as a replacement will generate resistance.

Framing it as:

\> a scalable \*product-level review layer\* for AI-era development

would likely get more buy-in.

\---

Strengths:

\* identifies real AI-era problem  
\* clear thinking  
\* innovative idea  
\* good discussion structure

Weaknesses:

\* underestimates implementation risks  
\* assumes extremely reliable automation  
\* reduces architecture and security oversight  
\* tooling complexity high
